title: Rounding Calculator - Round Numbers to Any Precision
tagline: Round any number to the nearest integer, decimal place, ten, hundred, or thousand — multiple rounding modes in one free tool.
intro: Enter a number and choose a rounding method. The calculator instantly applies your selected mode and displays the result.

about.heading: About the rounding calculator
about.body:
Rounding is the process of replacing a number with an approximation that has fewer significant digits, making it simpler to use while retaining enough precision for the task at hand. It is one of the most frequently applied arithmetic operations — used in everyday shopping, scientific measurement, financial reporting, engineering, and software development.

This rounding calculator supports ten distinct rounding modes. Round Half Up is the standard everyday method: when the deciding digit is exactly 5, the number rounds away from zero. For positive numbers, 2.5 rounds to 3 and 2.4 rounds to 2. Floor rounding (round down) always rounds toward negative infinity, so 2.9 becomes 2 and −2.1 becomes −3. Ceiling rounding (round up) always rounds toward positive infinity, so 2.1 becomes 3 and −2.9 becomes −2.

Rounding to the nearest 10, 100, or 1,000 is used when summarising large data sets, estimating quantities, or presenting numbers in reports where exact values would be distracting. Rounding to decimal places (1 dp, 2 dp, 3 dp) is essential for currency calculations, scientific measurements, and statistics — for example, rounding a measurement to 2 decimal places gives you centimetre-level precision when working in metres.

Choosing the right rounding mode matters. Use floor rounding when you need to guarantee a quantity is never overstated — for example, when calculating how many full boxes fit in a truck. Use ceiling rounding when you need to guarantee something is never understated — for example, how many buses are needed to transport a group of people. Use round-half-up for general arithmetic and when presenting results to a non-technical audience.

A subtle but important point is the difference between rounding a single number and accumulating rounding errors over many operations. In financial accounting, summing individually rounded line items can produce totals that differ from the rounded total of the exact sum by a cent or two. Banker's rounding (round half to even), which is not implemented here, was designed to reduce this bias. For everyday use, round-half-up is accurate enough, but if you are building a financial system, investigate the rounding rules mandated by your jurisdiction or accounting standard.

Whether you are a student checking your arithmetic, a developer verifying output, or an analyst preparing a report, this rounding calculator covers every common scenario. Select your mode, type your number, and get the answer instantly.

examples.heading: Rounding calculator examples
examples.intro: Representative examples showing different numbers rounded using several methods.

items.note: Rounded to 2 decimal places: the third decimal digit (5) causes the second decimal (6) to round up to 7.
Round Half Up to nearest integer: the 0.5 fraction rounds up to 3.
Floor (round down): always truncates to 2, ignoring the 0.5 fractional part.
Rounded to nearest 100: the tens digit (5) is ≥ 5, so 100 → 200.
Rounded to 3 decimal places: the fourth decimal digit is 5, which rounds the third decimal digit up from 1 to 2, giving 3.142.

howto.heading: How to use the rounding calculator
steps: Type any integer or decimal number into the 'Number to Round' input field.
Select the rounding method by clicking one of the mode buttons — for example, 'Round Half Up', '2 Decimal Places', or 'Nearest 100'.
Click Calculate. The result appears immediately below with the method name for reference.
Try a different rounding mode on the same number by clicking another method button and recalculating.
Click Reset to clear the input and start fresh with a new number.

faq.heading: Rounding calculator FAQ
faq.q1: What is the difference between floor and ceiling rounding?
faq.q1_a: Floor (round down) always moves toward negative infinity: 2.9 → 2 and −2.1 → −3. Ceiling (round up) always moves toward positive infinity: 2.1 → 3 and −2.9 → −2. Neither depends on the size of the fractional part — they always move in their respective directions regardless.
faq.q2: How do I round to 2 decimal places?
faq.q2_a: Select the '2 Decimal Places' mode. The calculator multiplies your number by 100, applies round-half-up, then divides by 100. For example, 3.14567 × 100 = 314.567, which rounds to 315, giving 3.15. This is the standard method used for monetary values.
faq.q3_a: Round half up (also called "round half away from zero" for positive numbers) is the everyday rounding rule most people learn in school: if the digit after the rounding position is 5 or more, round up; otherwise round down. So 2.5 → 3 and 2.4 → 2.
faq.q4: When should I use rounding to the nearest 10 or 100?
faq.q4_a: Use these modes when presenting summary statistics or estimates where fine-grained detail adds no value. For example, reporting a company's revenue as $4,600,000 rounded to the nearest hundred thousand communicates the magnitude clearly without distracting precision.
faq.q5_a: Rounding always introduces a small approximation. For a single number, the error is at most half of the rounding unit (e.g., ±0.5 for rounding to integers). When many rounded values are added together, individual errors can accumulate, which is why financial systems sometimes use banker's rounding to reduce systematic bias.
faq.q6: What is the difference between rounding and truncation?
faq.q6_a: Truncation simply drops digits after the chosen position, equivalent to always rounding toward zero. Floor rounding does the same for positive numbers but rounds toward negative infinity for negatives. Standard rounding (half-up) considers the first dropped digit and may round either way, making it a better approximation on average.
